text:
Okay, lots to unpack here. First of all, this actually is not using an emulator nor a modded ROM, it's the actual game itself, using Action Replay codes I made. 

I'm assuming that these days emulators can run those codes too (I'm not very informed about emulation, ironically), so that probably should work I guess. 

Second, all my codes (plus some other stuff from my website) are still for download in a zip file on media fire. The link is on my channel's main page in the channel description, so that stuff is still there. ;)

Third, in case you're wondering how the game was recorded or how I made those codes, I used something called an "Advanced Game Port" which is, like, a third-party Game Boy Player for Game Cube with built-in Action Replay and something called a "trainer software" to make your own codes.  (The "emulator issues" some have pointed out are due to it not being an "official Nintendo product", and if you want to get really nitpicky, the Advance Game Port is technically emulating a Game Boy Advance to play the real game cart inserted into it.)

And to "reveal my secrets", the very basic principle of a "trainer software" works like this:

Say, you're playing Super Mario 64. Mario has 5 lives, and you want to hack an "infinite lives" code. You open your trainer software, and search for "value = 5". This will find all memory addresses that currently have the value 5, though there'll be way too many at first to display them all.

Next, let Mario die, reducing his life count to 4. Now search for "value = 4". This will filter all previously found memory addresses that had value 5 to only leave those that now have the value 4 instead. Rinse and repeat this until there's only a few left, then check to see if any of them is a code for infinite lives. Voilà, no more Game Over for Mario.

A good trainer software will also allow you to search for less precise stuff like "value is larger than before" / "value is lower than before" / "value is the same as before" / "value is different than before". This is useful for, say, hacking a health bar where you have no idea what the exact value is. 

Oh, keep in mind that in programming, often counting will start at 0 instead of at 1, though that doesn't apply to the "infinite lives" example since "0 lives" is also a thing. Just something to keep in mind that sometimes you need to subtract 1 in your search. 

Infinite health is of course the most basic example, but this basic principle can also be applied to things that are a lot more interesting. Like what character you are playing as (which can unlock hidden unplayable characters), which level you are playing (which, again, can let you access otherwise unplayable areas), or in the case of Wario Ware, the "difficulty" setting. Just play under the three regular difficulties, and keep filtering for 0, 1, 2 with your trainer software. Once you got the code, you're no longer bound to those. ;)



Then, of course, modifying the memory address you found instead of the value can also lead to interesting discoveries since games often keep a lot of "similar stuff" close together in their memory. So taking a "infinite lives" code, say, 80123450:0005, and checking to see what happens at 80123451:0005 through 8012345F:0005 can also lead a lot of fun results. ;)

It's how I found a code in Bomberman 64 to make the player turn into a "ghost" in single player that can "possess" the final boss and make him run off the stage. (It's a thing in multiplayer normally.) :D

Never found its way into a video, but there's lots of wacky stuff like that I found. ^_^

text:
It significantly predates that sentiment, actually!

It was, in part, about being able to truthfully claim I had never even used an emulator (except for, like, twice, at a friend's place), and to clarify that this worked on the original games just by using Action Replay and the like, which back then, you could just buy on a store or online.

I guess the Advance Game Port does count as emulation in so much that it temporarily copies the GBA game cartridge's contents into the Game Cube's RAM to run it from there, and only read/write the cart to save/load save files after that. As soon as you turned the GCN off, it'd lose that ROM though. 


But yeah, if someone had ever complained, I could've just pointed out how none of what I was doing was even advertising emulation. ;p

text:
Aww, thanks! :D

"I doubt you'll ever read this"
Yeah Youtube's comment system isn't exactly built for really talking with people, unfortunately, just for abstract "engagement". :( 
But my phone notified me of your comment by chance, so I guess I'll have to reply to it immediately or else lose track of it since there isn't a way to "save" comments "for later" so you can reply to them when you've got something to say. - _-;


...Still, nice to hear you say that. :)

Maybe one day I'll make more videos, though it might not be like this, we'll see. I admit I was starting to run low on interesting hacking material to show off with the tools and games I had available to me, and what people can do these days is something I simply cannot compete with, my stuff is utterly mundane by comparison. O_O

...Though my style of editing is something I seldom see applied to that type of content, so there may be a niche I can try to explore one of these days. (Although no time soon, I've got... quite some other things to deal with for the foreseeable future... and the foreseeable future after that... and the foreseeable future after that.  ._.) 


Yeah, I like Vinesauce corruptions too, but you can tell that these days there's a drive towards quantity (“more watch time, make more videos faster, produce produce produce! we must please the algorithm, or else...!“), and my approach was always "quality over quantity". I crafted my videos to be rewatchable - that is to say, I'd watch them over and over again myself while making them, sometimes on a loop... This would highlight the boring bits and what didn't fit right. Then I'd take time away from that project and do the same thing a few days or weeks later, after the initial excitement of "durr, I made a thing" wore down a bit, thus once again making the parts that weren't quite right stand out more. Rinse repeat a few times, and eventually I'd call a video "done" and post it. "Quality over quantity"... or perfectionism, if you like. ^_^;;

...Heck, I never even uploaded all my videos and animations to this channel since I didn't want to flood it with my oldest, lesser-quality videos or unfitting ones like animated birthday greetings for people most of my subscribers wouldn't know, and, err ... "delude the quality of the channel"? ^__^;;;

One of these days I'll upload those as unlisted videos, put 'em in a playlist, and then post a public "Compilation" video with links to the playlist, or something. ;)

...I mean, they're already out there if you're interested, https://www.youtube.com/user/UltraTheHedgehog/videos and https://www.deviantart.com/ultrathehedgetoaster/gallery 
(Though tough luck finding the flash cartoons on a website that'll no longer tell you what's a regular image and what's just a preview-image for a flash animation you'll now need to download to watch since it no longer plays in-browser. Thanks, Adobe. -_-  I'd recommend using a gallery-downloader tool like gallery-dl.exe, that'll just download all the files for ya, including the *.swf ones.)

author: @ShyGuyXXL

text:
Well, I wouldn't have called it "modding". "Action Replay", Xploder, Gameshark etc have been around for a very long time (at least since NES, probably longer).

It's not rewriting game code, or creating brand new game content, or anything like that, it's all about narrowing down which memory addresses do what (or sometimes blindly poking around near known memory addresses and seeing what their "neighbours" do) , and then setting those addresses to a fixed value.

Compared to that, modding seems more impressive to me. I've never actually done that. ^_^;

Glad to hear I could mystify and inspire! ;)

text:
Hi,

first of all, since the shutdown of my website, all my codes are available here for download as a zip-archive: http://bit.ly/beyond-cheating
(or    https://www.mediafire.com/folder/ndgxqrakewc23/beyond-cheating   in case bit.ly fails at some point in the future)

The "/x-codez" directory contains all codes by me (except where stated otherwise), organized by console and game, while "/movie-codes" is what was used in the movies.

Second, since it's been a long while since I hacked stuff, or this game in particular, I'm not 100% sure on the details, but I guess this is what you want:

___________________________________________________________
Wario Ware

infinitive energy  (encrypted code for... Action Replay, I guess)
22DAB941 9DF4A9AD   

infinitive energy (decrypted version, I think? I mean, it looks about right.)
003039E5 00000003


difficulty modifier
003039E4 000000XX

speed modifier for minigames [NOTE #1]
10303886 00000xxx

speedmodifier for almost everything exept minigames  [NOTE #2]
203048b4 0000xxxx

(Set xxx to.... err, something. Sorry, I don't remember which values are the default here. :-/ )

NOTE #1:
The codes do not work for the games accessable directly from the menu, meaning games such as "Paper Plane", "Jump Forever" etc..

NOTE #2:
Using "speedmodifier for almost everything exept minigames" with a very low value will give you almost infinitive time during a minigame. HOWEVER, you NEED to deactivate the codes before starting the game, else you won't get past the startscreen (or probably not even past the Nintendo-logo).
___________________________________________________________

There's a little more stuff in the codes files in the bit.ly/beyond-cheating zip-archive including "encrypted" versions of the speed codes that can be triggered via button-press.



Thirdly, - this probably won't be relevant, but I figure I'd ask - what type of device are you using to hack? I'm asking because I want to know if you need the codes "encrypted" (something that looks more like the encrypted infinite energy code up there, "22DAB941 9DF4A9AD") or "decrypted" (something like "003039E4 000000XX" where "XX" can be freely replaced). 

If you're using an emulator it's probably "decrypted". (My experience with emulators is severely limited, sorry. ^_^;;;)

It took me a bit to remember, but I think the Game Boy Advance Action Replay used to use "encrypted" codes, while the "Advance Game Port" (which I used to make these; it's like a Game Boy Player for Game Cube a with built-in GBA Action Replay plus "Cheat Construction Kit" trainer-software) uses decrypted codes.

...The whole encrypted/decrypted thing was, like, a shitty copyright mechanism the company that made the Action Replay employed so their codes wouldn't be used on their competitor's cheat devices. However there's a fan-made tool to convert between the two, "AR Crypt V2.4b" (for GBA) is the version I still have lying around on my computer.


...Again, this last part probably isn't relevant to you but I'd already started looking into it because I genuinely got confused not initially being able to recall why some of my own codes were encrypted. ^_^;;;;

SPECIAL NOTE CONCERNING JOKERCOMMANDS: 
Seeing that the Advance Game Port (the GBA-hackingdevice I'm using) does not appear to support jokercommands (also known as "activator codes"),
I myself am unable to confirm if the jokercommand-toggled codes work as intended.
If you don't know what jokercommands/activatorcodes are, well they let you activate/deactivate a code during the game by pressing a button.
Some codes need jokercommands or else they are either completely useless (a moonjump without a jokercommand can not be controlled) or they will crash the game. 
If these jokercommand-codes do NOT work on your Action Replay Version 3, please inform me.

text:
Well, there of course aren't any settings, but lots of other data instead.
And basically this is what happens, the game mistakes random garbage data for the minigames' difficulty settings.

I love it when games do this. ^w^

It mostly works on GBA / GB(C).
Another example for this kind of stuff probably are the "Virus-Pokémon", such as x##'M## which comes along with Missingno..
(You remember, that pixely square-like character in the Pokémon games.)
author: @UltramasterBDJ

text:
which means, the minigames' settings for each difficulty are stored in equidistant addresses ( = each of the addresses having the same distance);

like e.g. the settings for difficulty level 1 are stored at e.g. from addresses 123000 to 123100, for level 2 at 124000 to 124100, for level 3 at 125000 to 125100 or so.
Now what would happen if the game would try to load level 4 (within my example, of course)?
It would search for the settings at 126000.
author: @UltramasterBDJ

text:
How can there be a 1638th level?
That can easily be explained:


For each minigame in each difficulty, there are settings. 
I couldn't tell for sure, but I assume at least some of the minigames use a 
mathematical formula to extrapolate these settings (how many enemies and so on) for different difficulties. 
Increasing the value for the difficulty beyond 3 will then cause insane results.

Either that, or, instead each minigames' settings are being addressed by a "pointer" or somethin',

author: @UltramasterBDJ

text:
Speedmod - game goes continuously faster; search for greater value. Reset game, search for lower value.

Difficulty-mod: I noticed that the game has three different difficulties for each minigame. So, when it has the lowest difficulty search for 0, the middle difficulty search for 1, the highest difficulty search for 2.

Tada, difficulty-modifier found.
...uhh... the basic knowledge I mentioned before AND some creativity to come up with ideas what to hack.
